Why “just use a GPU” stopped being good enough advice
A few years ago, if someone asked which chip you needed for an AI project, the answer was almost always the same: get a GPU. That advice still works, but it’s no longer complete. Today’s AI stack runs on at least six different kinds of processors, each one built to solve a problem the others are bad at. If you’re a developer, an architect, or just someone trying to make sense of why your cloud bill has line items for “TPU pods” and “inference accelerators,” it helps to know what each chip is actually for.
This isn’t a chip-design lecture. It’s a practical map: what each processor does, where it shines, where it falls apart, and when you’d actually reach for it.
The processor zoo, in one sentence each
Before going deep, here’s the short version:
CPU handles general logic and orchestration. GPU brute-forces parallel math for training. TPU is Google’s purpose-built tensor cruncher for large-scale deep learning. NPU brings AI inference on-device, in your phone or laptop, without touching the cloud. LPU is built for fast, predictable token generation in LLM inference. DPU offloads networking, storage, and security so the other chips don’t have to.
